coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of picric acid based on creatinine-capped solid film assembled by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and chitosan
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shufan
Song, Yu
Shi, Fanping
Liu, Yunling
Ma, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Picric acid (PA) was sensitively detected via changed fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) solid film in this paper. The mixture of N-GQDs and chitosan was firstly deposited on the silica slides to form N-GQDs solid film. Then creatinine was capped outside the solid film to enhance the selectivity to PA according to Jaffé reaction. The prepared solid film can provide strong fluorescence signal due to the strong blue emission of N-GQDs. PA can quench the fluorescence signal of creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan solid film greatly due to the electron transfer among PA, creatinine and N-GQDs. Under the optimal condition, the fluorescence intensity of solid film was related linearly to the concentration of PA in the range of 2.0–200.0ng/mL with a detection limit of 1.24ng/mL. The selectivity of fluorescence quenching of the fluorescent solid film for PA was enhanced. Moreover, the potential application of the proposed methodology for the sensing of PA in water was demonstrated with a satisfactory result. The fluorescence intensity of film sensor was investigated and showed high stability in four weeks. To the best of our knowledge, it was the first report of PA detection by using the quenched fluorescence of N-GQDs solid film.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Picric acid
Solid film
Creatinine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of fluorescent film toward PA
Selectivity and stability of the fabricated solid film
Interference study and analysis of PA in water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China , People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China , People's Republic of China (nos. 21075050 and 21005029 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the BSA/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan solid film sensor for PA detection and the schematic view of the jaffé reaction.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs. Inset: photograph image of N-GQDs solution under UV lamp (365nm). (B) FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of glass plate (a), chitosan/glass plate (b), N-GQDs-chitosan (c), creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan (d), BSA/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan (e), and with the addition of 100ng/ml PA (f). Inset: photograph image of film sensor (g), with the presence of PA (h) under 365nm UV lamp.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSA/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan solid film in the presence of PA at 0 (red line I0), 2, 5, 10, 20, 80, 100, 150, 200ng/mL (from top to bottom). (B) Stern-Volmer curve. I and I0 were the fluorescence intensities of BSA/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan fluorescent solid film at 360nm in the presence and absence of PA, respectively. (C) Fluorescence photographs of the solid film probe with presence of PA at 0, 2, 40, 120, 200, 500ng/mL (from a to f) taken under 365nm UV illumination. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of the N-GQDs solid film for the PA analysis.

Fluorescence quenching efficiency toward BSA/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan monolayer film after the addition of 50ng/mL of various analytes (I and I0 were the fluorescence intensities of solid film in the presence and absence of analytes, respectively).
Detection of spiked PA in water samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of picric acid based on creatinine-capped solid film assembled by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and chitosan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Picric acid (PA) was sensitively detected via changed fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) solid film in this paper. The mixture of N-GQDs and chitosan was firstly deposited on the silica slides to form N-GQDs solid film. Then creatinine was capped outside the solid film to enhance the selectivity to PA according to Jaffé reaction. The prepared solid film can provide strong fluorescence signal due to the strong blue emission of N-GQDs. PA can quench the fluorescence signal of creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan solid film greatly due to the electron transfer among PA, creatinine and N-GQDs. Under the optimal condition, the fluorescence intensity of solid film was related linearly to the concentration of PA in the range of 2.0–200.0ng/mL with a detection limit of 1.24ng/mL. The selectivity of fluorescence quenching of the fluorescent solid film for PA was enhanced. Moreover, the potential application of the proposed methodology for the sensing of PA in water was demonstrated with a satisfactory result. The fluorescence intensity of film sensor was investigated and showed high stability in four weeks. To the best of our knowledge, it was the first report of PA detection by using the quenched fluorescence of N-GQDs solid film.

ce:section.ce:para: The well organized terror attacks and increased use of modern bombs made the detection of explosives become an important problems and prompted researchers to find more effective ways to detect explosives. Nitroaromatic compounds (NACs) as trinitrotoluene (TNT), dinitrotoluene (DNT), and picric acid (PA) were primary constituents of the explosives. Among these compounds, TNT was the most popular choice. However, PA also emerged as a potential substitute due to its superior explosive power. Moreover, the extensive application of PA in dye industries, fireworks, rocket fuel, organic synthesis and drugs analysis imposed severe risks on human safety and environment health [1–3]. PA at low concentration can do massive damage to human eyes, skin and respiratory systems, and cause contamination of ground water [4,5]. Therefore, various techniques have existed for the detection of PA, such as flow injection analysis [6], mass spectrometry [7], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [8], X-ray diffraction [9], electrochemistry[10], fluorescence sensing [11–15]. Among the reported analytical methods, fluorescence signaling was one of the best choices for the detection of nitroaromatics due to its high determination sensitivity and selectivity [14]. To date, fluorescent chemosensors [16,17], metal-organic framework [18] and fluorescent polymer[19,20] have been explored for PA detection. However, the methods were considered to be deficient such as time-consuming synthesis or bring secondary pollution. Thus, novel and easily available fluorescent probes for monitoring ultra-trace levels of PA were urgently needed.

Carbon-based nanomaterials including graphene oxide, carbon nanodots, and carbon nanotubes have received intensive attention for their sensing application due to their properties like low toxicity, high electrical and thermal conductivity, robust chemical inertness, good bio-compatibility [21–23]. Due to these unique properties, carbon-based nanomaterials showed high potential to cover requirements for producing selective, sensitive, simple and low cast sensors for trace PA detection. For example, Dinda et al. and Huang et al. fabricated sensing systems of PA using the functionalized reduced graphene oxide [24,4]. Deng and Wu detected the 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (PA) successfully according to the photoluminescence energy transfer between carbon nanodots and PA [25]. In addition, Sadhanala and Nanda reported a new photoluminescent probe based on the synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanoparticles (BN-CNPs) [11]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new style of carbon-based nanomaterials, were getting increasing study in the recent past, owing to optical stability, good surface grafting, and fascinating electronic character. However, the low quantum yield of GQDs limited their application range. Doping GQDs with N atoms, having a comparable atomic size and five valence electrons for bonding with carbon atoms, can drastically alter their electronic characteristics and offer more active sites, thus producing new phenomena and unexpected properties [26,27]. Based on this novel nanomaterial, 6-mercaptopurine, Hg2+, H2O2, glucose, glutathione, cysteine and sulfite acquired successful determination [28–32]. But most detection with carbon-based nanomaterials processed in aqueous solution system as homogeneous phase analysis. In the past decades, the heterogeneous phase analysis received great interest due to their distinct advantages over homogeneous phase analysis, The heterogeneous phase analysis can diminish strong interference signal from coexisting substances in the matrix solution, which is extremely favorable for trace analysis [33]. Furthermore, the heterogeneous phase analysis can be used for vapor/gas detection. Recently, fluorescent solid films consisting of various kinds of luminophores have been reported in the heterogeneous phase analysis applications. The solid films are easily stored and transported as a result of a good chemical stability of luminophores in the solid state [33]. And solid film-based detection enables real-space and real-time measurements [34].

In this paper, the mixture of chitosan solution and N-GQDs solution was deposited on the silica plate firstly fabricating the solid film. Chitosan is a well-known biocompatible and biodegradable natural polysaccharide with low toxicity and can easily form films. One extra advantage of using chitosan as a film sensor support is that it is rich in free amine functionalities, which can be used to chemically anchor fluorescent reporter units [35]. And in terms of as-prepared N-GQDs, there exist large amount carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on its surface [36]. Therefore, chitosan and N-GQDs can be closely integrated with each other under electrostatic interaction. As all the NACs are highly electron deficient in nature due to presence of nitro groups, the PL quenching easily occurs when interacts with electron rich fluorophores [11,24]. Then creatinine and BSA were coated outside the N-GQDs-chitosan film successively (Scheme 1 ). According to the classical Jaffé reaction, creatinine can react with PA and form Janovsky complex in alkaline environment which can enhance the selectivity of the solid film [37]. BSA was reported to be the good blocking agent which can block the non-specific absorption of target sensing object. And BSA protein can be conjugated to chitosan surface to improve the physiological stability of chitosan film [38–41]. It was first report for PA detection by utilizing N-GQDs based solid film. And also, the proposed solid film sensor provided a convenient, low-cost, green, selective and sensitive method for PA detection, which could be an alternative technique in the potential application of the volatile organic compounds analysis.

Citric acid, ammonia (30% wt), carboxymethyl chitosan (90%), acetonitrile (ACN), bovine serum albumin (BSA), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), H2O2 (30%), ethanol, methanol, benzene, methylbenzene, hydroquinone, NaOH, BaCl2, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, ZnCl2, and CaCl2 were purchased from Beijing Dingguo Chemicals Co. (Beijing, China). Picric acid (PA), 2,4-dinitraniline, 2-nitroaniline, p-nitrophenol, 2,4-dinitrotolune, 4-nitrochlorobenzene and creatinine were obtained from Aldrich Chemical Co. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Water used in study had a resistivity higher than 18MΩcm. The water samples were collected from the underground water in Jilin University. All pH measurements were taken with a PHS-3C pH meter (Tuopu Co., Hangzhou, China). Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was conducted on a Thermo Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrometer using KBr pellets. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired on a UV-1200 UV–vis Spectrophotometer with a 1cm quart cell. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with a Thermo ESCALAB VG Scientific 250 spectrometer equipped with monochromatized Al Kα excitation. All fluorescent spectra were surveyed on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer (150W xenon lamp) with a 1cm path-length quartz cell at room temperature.

N-GQDs were synthesized by carbonization of CA with ammonia according to the previous reports [27]. 2g of citric acid and 0.3ml of ammonia were transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 210°C for 1h. The resultant dark brown mixture (2g) was dissolved to 10ml with ultrapure water. Subsequently, pH of N-GQDs dispersion was tuned to 7.0 with adding NaOH solution dropwise.The supernatant was collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation at 12000rpm for 10min. Then the obtained liquid was diluted to 20ml with water acquiring N-GQDs stock solution with concentration of 0.1g/ml. Finally, the as-prepared N-GQDs were stored at 4°C for further characterization and use.

First, we activated silica plates used in experiment by treating them in a “piranha solution” (3/7, v/v, 30% H2O2/98% H2SO4) and boiling for 30min [42]. Then the plates were cleaned in pure water with sonication for 30min, followed by thorough rinsing with copious amounts of water and drying in nitrogen atmosphere. The next step, the mixture of N-GQDs and carboxymethyl chitosan was prepared. 0.5g of chitosan was dissolved in 19.6ml of water and 0.4ml of N-GQDs stock solution was added. The final concentration of N-GQDs was 0.002g/ml in the mixed solution. N-GQDs with rich negative charged hydroxyl and carboxy, and chitosan can integrate with each other due to the electrostatic interaction. The silica plate was immersed into the N-GQDs-chitosan solution at room temperature and dried in air. The homogeneous, dense N-GQDs-chitosan film was deposited on silica substrate successfully. Creatinine (1mM) and BSA (2% w/v) were capped outside the film successively by using the above-mentioned procedure, thus yielding BAS/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan fluorescent solid film. In this way, a novel sensor for PA detection was established completely.

The PA stock solution (100mg/L) was prepared by dissolving PA in ACN. Serious concentrations of PA liquor were prepared by adding different volumes of PA stock solution to NaOH solution. Then the fabricated film sensor was immersed in PA/NaOH solutions with different concentrations and the fluorescent spectra of BAS/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan film were collected in solid phase at room temperature. All fluorescence spectra were recorded with 5/5 slid width and 360nm excitation wavelength. Quantification of PA in water samples was performed by the method of standard addition.

To study the spectral properties of the N-GQDs, the fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were acquired from the aqueous dispersion of N-GQDs as illustrated in Fig. 1 A. And the optimal excitation wavelength (353nm) and emission wavelength (440nm) of the as-synthesized N-GQDs can be obtained from spectra. From the photograph depicted in the inset of Fig. 1A, it can be found that the diluted N-GQDs aqueous solution exhibited a bright blue color under the illumination of UV lamp (365nm). The FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 1B were used to identify the surface functional groups present on the resultant N-GQDs. The absorption bands at 3439cm−1 and 3062cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The peak at 1376cm−1 was from the bending vibration of CNH and indicated the successful adulteration of nitrogen atoms into the GQDs [43]. The strong absorption band at 1706cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of CO, and peaks at 1178cm−1 and 1231cm−1 related to the COH stretching vibrations, implying the oxygen-rich property of the N-GQDs. These functional groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of the N-GQDs in an aqueous system, which accounted for the great potential of the material for sensing in aqueous samples [44].

To further explored the optical properties of the N-GQDs, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs were studied (Fig. S1). In the UV–vis absorption of N-GQDs, a strong absorption peak at 334nm can be observed. It was assigned to the n-π* transition of the CO bone, which can result in strong fluorescence [30]. The fluorescence spectra in Fig. S1 were collected under different excitation wavelengths. It can be seen that the emission peak showed red-shift from 440 to 540nm by increasing the excitation wavelength from 330 to 480nm. Such excitation-dependent fluorescent behavior was related to the different surface states and size distribution of the N-GQDs [36,44]. Meanwhile, the narrow and symmetrical fluorescence spectra profiles suggest the narrower size distribution of the obtained N-GQDs [36,43]. The surface composition and elemental analysis for the resultant nanoparticles were determined by XPS. The survey XPS spectrum in Fig. S2A showed three peaks at 284.58, 400.75 and 531.34eV, which can be attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively [21]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of the N-GQDs (Fig. S2B) can be deconvolved into four peaks at 284.39, 285.11, 286.53 and 288.14eV, which confirmed the presence of CC, CN, CO and CO groups [28]. The results indicated that there existed O-rich groups in N-GQDs, which was consistent with the corresponding FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2B). The N 1s peaks at 399.42 and 400.22eV shown in Fig. S2C indicated that nitrogen existed mostly in the form of C NC and N(C)3, respectively, implying that N has been partly doped into the GQDs [27]. The two fitted peaks at 531.34 and 532.40eV in O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2D) were assigned to CO and COH/ COC groups, respectively. The XPS analyses clearly demonstrate the successful preparation of N-GQDs.

In order to verify the feasibility of the experiment, a series of fluorescence measurements were carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 , both silica plate (curve a) and chitosan/silica plate (curve b) did not have any fluorescence emission. Curve c to e were the fluorescence spectra of every step of fabrication of the proposed fluorescent solid film. The fluorescence intensity of solid film increased largely with the addition of N-GQDs (from b to c). And after capping the N-GQDs-chitosan film with creatinine and BSA, the intensity had a slight decrease due to the shielding effect (from c to e). When contacted with PA solution (100ng/mL), the fluorescence intensity of film can be quenched to 47% (curve f). And from Fig. 2, it can be seen that the fluorescence emission of film sensor was absolutely from the N-GQDs and PA had an excellent quenching efficiency. Subsequently, the assembled time of N-GQDs-chitosan film/silica plate was investigated and results were showed in Fig. S3. The fluorescence intensity of solid film enhanced with the increase of the assembled time. The surface of silica plate equipped with massive Si-OH after the activation in “piranha solution”. N-GQDs-chitosan can be attached to silica plate due to possession of plentiful amino groups. And fluorescence intensity reached to maximum when silica substrate was covered of N-GQDs-chitosan. These results indicated that the binding sites of silica plate surface were occupied by N-GQDs-chitosan completely when assembled time reach to 5min. Then five minutes was chosen as assembled time in process of fabricating N-GQDs-chitosan film. The optimal pH value for sensing PA was discussed in Fig. S4. The effect of pH on quenching efficiency of PA was investigated from pH 7–12. Fig. S4 showed the fluorescence spectra of BSA/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan in the absence/presence of PA. From Fig. S4A, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of solid film kept stable from pH 7–12. It was because that the solid film had high stability after BSA coating in weak alkaline environment. Fig. S4B showed that the fluorescence intensity of the proposed solid film with the addition of PA varied greatly under different pH values. As shown in Fig. S4C, the maximum quenching effect of PA on the solid film occurred at pH 11. It indicated that reaction between creatinine and PA needed an alkaline medium [45,46]. The reaction took place through the interaction of PA with the creatinine carbanion. So, a high pH condition was needed to convert the creatinine on the surface of solid film to its carbanion form [47]. Then pH of 11 was selected to be the optimal pH value in PA sensing procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of fluorescent film toward PA
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3 A displayed the fluorescence spectra of solid film with the presence of various concentrations of PA. The fluorescence emission intensity of solid film was continuously decreased with the increasing concentration of PA. The quenched fluorescence can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation,
(1)
I
I
0
= 0.8748 +
K
S V
[ P A ]
where [PA] was the concentration of the quencher. And KSV was the stern-volmer constant. I0 and I were the fluorescence intensity of fluorescent solid film in the absence and presence of PA. The liner relationship (R=0.998) was observed for the concentration of PA ranging from 2.0 to 200.0ng/mL. And KSV was calculated to be −3.4×10−3 (ng/mL)−1 (Fig. 3B). The detection limit (LOD) was 1.24ng/mL, which was defined by the equation LOD=3σ/s. s is the slope of the calibration curve. σ is the standard deviation of the corrected blank signals of the proposed film. A series of noticeable luminance change of prepared solid film sensor can be observed under a UV lamp (Fig. 3C). Nitroaromatic compounds, such as PA, are electron deficient analytes and can act as electron acceptors for photoexcited electrons of the N-GQDs. Based on mechanistic data of Jaffé reaction, the interaction between the BSA/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan film and PA can be postulated as follows. Under alkaline conditions, creatinine on the surface of monolayer film was converted to a crbanion through the abstration of a proton from its methylene carbon. And the active methylene compound reacted with PA to form the Janovsky complex [47].
(2) creatinine/N− GQDs +OH− →creatinine− +H2O
(3) creatinine− + PA→ Janovskycomplex
Therefore, an electron-transfer donor-acceptor mechanism can be responsible for the observed fluorescence quenching of the N-GQDs-creatinine nanoparticles when the N-GQDs-creatinine-PA complex was formed [47].

To shed some light on the mechanism that was responsible for quenching of fluorescence of solid film, the fluorescence lifetime of solid film in the absence and presence of PA were measured (Fig. 4 ). It can be seen that fluorescence lifetime of the established solid film became shorter with the addition of PA than the solid film alone. The average lifetime reduced from 7.41ns to 7.16ns (Table S1), indicating that the dynamic quenching process was dominant for the quenching mechanism. In the fabricated solid film, the creatinine can absorb PA selectively through the formation of Janovskycomplex. And then electron transferred from electron-rich N-GQDs to the electron-deficient PA which led to the fluorescence quenching of solid film [36,48,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the fabricated solid film
ce:section.ce:para: Among the nitroaromatics analytes, the quenching becomes better when aromatic analytes have more electron-deficient groups [12]. So, there were more nitro groups on aromatic ring, the fluorescence quenching was more intensive. Therefore, the fluorescence solid film has high sensitivity to detect PA. Fluorescence quenching experiments of the BSA/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan upon addition of different nitroaromatic compounds were conducted to confirm the selectivity of the method for PA. The quenching response obtained for the analytes at a concentration of 50ng/mL was presented in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that PA had a much higher quenching efficiency (about 30%) than other structurally related analytes (lower than 10%). The quenching efficiency increased with the nitration degree of the molecule. Greater quenching behavior for PA as compared the other three nitroaromatic compounds can be explained by the higher electron accepting character due to the presence of a greater number of nitro groups bound at the benzene ring. We also investigated the optical stability of the solid film in four weeks and showed results in Fig. S5. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of solid film had no obvious decrease in 28days. This optical stability can be ascribed to the stable optical properties of N-GQDs. Moreover, the optical stability offered the proposed solid film a much longer service lifetime, which made the proposed method be more convenient, speedy and widely used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference study and analysis of PA in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the validity and test the selectivity of the film sensor for PA, a systematic study of various common metal ions was added into the system when the determination of PA (100ng/mL) was carried out under the optimum conditions. A relative error of ±5.0% was considered to be tolerable. As shown in Table S2, the tolerable concentration ratios of coexisting substances were over 500 fold for Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+ and Cl−, 200 fold for PO4 3−, ethanol and methanol. With these high concentrations of commonly existing substances, there was little interference on the fluorescence of the BSA/creatinine/N-GQDs-chitosan solid film. It was apparent that the novel film sensor was sensitive and selective for PA detection. In order to assess the feasibility of the proposed method, we applied the film sensor in water samples. The results obtained by standard addition were shown in Table 1 . The recoveries of PA in the real samples were between 96.27% and 101.6%. The results illustrated that the proposed film sensor for PA sensing was accurate and can be applied for a real samples assay.

In conclusion, it has been demonstrated the potential application of a fluorescence N-GQDs solid film probe to develop a new fluorescence sensor for the detection of PA. The fluorosensor was based on two facts: first, the selective interaction between PA and creatinine achieved by the Jaffé reaction. And secondly, a fluorescence quenching was achieved when PA was added. The selectivity study of the proposed method showed PA generated a more intense signal response to solid film than other nitromatic compounds owing to its higher electron-accepting character. A good linearity for PA detection in the range of 2.0–200.0ng/mL was found. Moreover, the potential application of the new analytical method was also demonstrated and got a satisfactory result. This was the first time that PA sensing had been based on N-GQDs fluorescent solid film. The film sensor provided a convenient, green, sensitive and rapid route for PA detection and can be extended to other environmental matrices of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, People's Republic of China (nos. 21075050 and 21005029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.03.071.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

J. Lumin.

Anal. Chim. Acta
Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Anal. Chem.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Simultaneous determination of creatinine and creatine in human serum by double-spike isotope dilution liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS)
Anal. Chem.
Development of a creatinine ELISA and an amperometric antibody-based creatinine sensor with a detection limit in the nanomolar range
Anal. Chem.
Determination of TNT explosive based on its selectively interaction with creatinine-capped CdSe/ZnS quantum dots
Anal. Chim. Acta

RSC Adv.

Nanoscale

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shufan Chen is currently doing her M.S. at the School of Chemistry, University of Jilin. Her research interest is the synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for the sensor applications.
Yu Song is currently doing her M.S. at the School of Chemistry, University of Jilin. Her research interest is the synthesis of ZnTe/ZnSe quantum dots for the sensor application.

Fanping Shi is currently doing her Ph.D. at the School of Chemistry, University of Jilin. Her research interest is the synthesis and functionalization of quantum dots and their application on sensors for metalion and small molecules.

Yunling Liu is a professor in the State Key laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University. He received his Ph.D. in 2000 at Jilin University (China). His research interest is the design and synthesis of functional coordination polymer materials.

Qiang Ma is an associate professor in the School of Chemistry, University of Jilin. He received his Ph.D. in 2008 at Jilin University (China). His research interest is the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
